attributes.stem: A 55-year-old man is brought into the Emergency Department (ED) by ambulance after being involved in a motor vehicle accident where he sustained a soft tissue wound deep to the back of his left leg. An X-ray shows a tibial shaft fracture which is reduced in ED with application of a long backslab. The patient is taken to theatre that night for definitive treatment with external fixation. He is reviewed in the ward several hours after the procedure as he has increasing pain in the leg, despite being given analgesia according to the standard protocol. The patient is agitated, with a blood pressure of 156/90 mmHg and pulse 104/min. There is a positive passive stretch test, but no associated paraesthesia or paralysis and the pedal pulses are palpable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
The concern must be the possibility of the development of a compartment syndrome. This can be a devastating condition where osseofacial compartment pressure rises to a level that decreases perfusion to muscle and nerves within the affected compartment. The most common situation where this develops is in long bones fractures where local trauma results in bleeding and oedema, leading to increased interstitial pressure, vascular occlusion and if prolonged, myoneural ischaemia. The diagnosis is based primarily on the physical assessment of a patient with intact mental status and where the pain out of proportion to the clinical situation is the main symptom. Of the classical 5 'P's (pain, paraesthesia, pallor, pulselessness and paralysis), pain is the earliest sign of compartment syndrome. Without intervention, this condition can lead to irreversible tissue injury and Volkmann's contracture.

Treatment on the ward includes the removal of all circumferential dressings and plasters. The leg is not to be elevated as this reduces perfusion to the extremities. Adequate analgesia should be given. Prompt contact must be made with the orthopaedic team who may well need to perform an emergency fasciotomy.

Intra-muscular pressure measurement is usually not performed with exceptions such as an inconclusive physical examination. If performed, delta pressure is calculated by subtracting compartment pressure from the diastolic pressure. The normal intra-compartmental pressure varies between 0 - 8 mmHg. Any pressure >30 mmHg is diagnostic of compartment syndrome and the need for fasciotomy. An increase in intra-compartmental pressure to within 10 - 30 mmHg of the patient's diastolic blood pressure indicates inadequate perfusion and relative tissue ischaemia

CT/MRI is indicated in patients who are unresponsive, uncooperative or have underlying peripheral nerve deficits.

Intravenous antibiotics are not indicated in compartment syndrome as there is no infection.
learningPoints: Compartment syndrome is an orthopaedic emergency with the diagnosis based largely on history and physical examination.
Early recognition and intervention in compartment syndrome are crucial to prevent irreversible tissue damage.
references: Ojike NI, Roberts CS, Giannoudis PV. Compartment syndrome of the thigh: a systematic review. Injury. 2010 Feb;41(2):133-6. doi: 10.1016/j.injury.2009.03.016. Epub 2009 Jun 24. PMID: 19555950. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19555950/

Garner MR, Taylor SA, Gausden E, Lyden JP. Compartment syndrome: diagnosis, management, and unique concerns in the twenty-first century. HSS J. 2014 Jul;10(2):143-52. doi: 10.1007/s11420-014-9386-8. Epub 2014 Jun 7. PMID: 25050098; PMCID: PMC4071472. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4071472/

Torlincasi AM, Lopez RA, Waseem M. Acute Compartment Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jan 16]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448124/
